title: How I Survived Being Thrown In A Russian Prison
text: I'm bald and bankrupt, and as regular viewers of the channel will no doubt already know, I've been arrested, I've been interrogated, and forced to publicly [snorts] apologize quite a few times over the years whilst making YouTube videos. It's um all part of the job, you could say. Or at least, it seems to be a part of my job. Anyway, today I'm going to break down three of my most infamous arrests, how I offended all of Chechnya, how I got arrested at a Russian cosmodrome, and finally, how I almost got a 7-year flipping sentence in a Siberian gulag. Are you ready to hear the truth behind the international headlines? If you are, then join me. >> [music] >> We might as well begin with my very first arrest and public apology that I had in my YouTube career. There's There's been quite a few of them since. Too many, in fact, for my liking. This one took place in the beautiful and actually very friendly uh Republic of Chechnya, down in the Russian Caucuses, a mysterious land on the very distant edge of Europe that has had its share of violence and upheaval, you could say, over the years, which has definitely bred a very, very tough population. Tough, but to be honest, also very hospitable. I like the Chechens, but we um we almost had a major falling out over something that seemed, well, to me at least, very, very trivial at the time. I was traveling through the region making videos that I hoped would show the outside world that the Islamic part of Russia It was now totally safe and actually very welcoming and open to foreigners. The people in the North Caucasus, whether it be Chechens, Dagestanis, um Ingush, or the Christian Ossetians, are by and large great people in my experience. Probably my favorite of all the Russian peoples, you could say. But on an overnight train ride to Grozny, the capital of Chechnya, I was alone in my cozy little cabin watching the snowy peaks of the Caucasus pass by the window. When in a moment of Lermontov romanticism, I flippantly commented to the camera that what I felt would make the the journey and the video extra special would be if there were Chechen girl in my cabin to share the overnight ride with. Well, I didn't actually say girl. That was the problem. I should have just stuck with girl, but instead I said Chechen chick. A bit crass in a conservative region, I do agree, but hardly overly vulgar. I've said far worse, as you guys know, in other parts of the world. After delivering the line, I stopped recording, closed the camera, and went to sleep thinking absolutely no more of it. Hmm, it gets [clears throat] interesting now. The next morning I reached Grozny in Chechnya, the capital. Checked into a cheap hostel and uploaded the video to YouTube with the chick comment in the video, and went about my day thinking no more of it. However, the following afternoon, I was somewhere in the mountains filming in a a remote village, you could say, about, I don't know, 2 hours, something like that, from Grozny, when suddenly a very expensive black Mercedes pulled up. The driver told me that I was being invited to the capital to meet someone at a ministry building. Oh, a ministry building, I wonder what happens there, I thought to myself. You see, at that time, back then, my channel was super popular, and I presumed, maybe slightly inflated ego, who knows, that I was being invited for a chat with some government bigwig about tourism in Chechnya and how I might promote it. And so, of course, I accepted this invitation. You'd be a fool not to. And on the drive down to the ministry building, I was imagining what riches the Chechen government were going to pay me for helping them. Perhaps I was going to get a Russian passport or the keys to the city of Grozny or maybe just a a nice steaming bowl of laghman. I'm so sorry. When I eventually [snorts] bloody arrived at the ministry building, I want to tell you, I was met by a guard who led me inside to a plush waiting room of leather armchairs. A beautiful secretary in traditional dress offered me tea. Oh, say beautiful secretary, no more. Until eventually the door to an office swung open and a man came out wearing the typical suit of Kadyrov cadres. You know what they look like? Like got a little collar here, black, some little symbol here. He introduced [snorts] himself as the minister of something or other. I don't remember now exactly what. He ushered me into his office, which is overlooked overlooked by an oil painting of Ramzan Kadyrov himself, the infamous Instagram-loving Republic of the president of the republic, I should say. And I took a seat waiting for the minister to reel off some statistics about tourism numbers and how I could help increase them. I still didn't realize at that point, to be honest with you, that I was totally in the >> [laughter] >> in a place you probably don't want to be in the  in. It's Chechnya, flipping heck. Anyway, the conversation didn't start as I expected. Instead of telling me how much of a fan of the channel the minister was and how honored he was to have me in the republic, he was telling me about other YouTubers that visited Chechnya before and that they were welcome to visit the republic, but they had to respect Chechen Chechen traditions. "Do you respect Chechen traditions, Benjamin?" the minister asked in his Chechen accent, not my northern accent. I shifted nervously in my seat. Sure, I replied, and I meant it. Then the minister pulled out his phone and showed me a clip of my video where I said the word chick. So what is this then? Again, in a Chechen accent, not in my accent. You want to share your cabin with a Chechen chick. Calling a Chechen girl chick is absolutely forbidden, he said. I'm not joking. I honestly thought that this was like some kind of flipping Candid Camera Chechen TV show. Maybe a Chechen Jeremy Beadle would step out of the cupboard with a little hand or something like that. Brits will know what I'm talking about. There was no way, I thought, someone could be that angry over a word like chick. I mean, come on. But he wasn't finished. It got a lot worse. Outside on the street is a mob that if you go out there now will tear you apart, he promised. Which I must say, um, I didn't like the sound of too much, to be honest with you. I mean, I was imagining a hundred wrestlers with leather jackets and cauliflower ears all waiting for me on the streets of Grozny. Um, how can I avoid the mob? I asked him, trying to sound as nonchalant as possible. I mean, nobody wants one Chechen enemy, right? Let alone flipping half of Grozny. >> [laughter] >> Anyway, after a pause, he said calmly, Benjamin, there is a way. Which, let me tell you, was, um, quite a relief. Benjamin, you must say sorry. And so, over the next hour, myself and the minister practiced how I was going to apologize to the Chechen nation for the heinous crime of saying the word chick. Place your hand on your heart when you say this bit for extra deference, he instructed, as he filmed my performance on his phone. I mean, look, surely he knew, as I knew, that it was all a farce and just a power play over fake offense. I mean, I know enough Chechens who use words worse than chick to describe a Russian girl, for example, but um I kept my thoughts to myself. Discretion, the better part of valor, and I didn't really want to be thrown off the balcony to the mob. After three or four takes, we had it in the bag. My apology to the people was ready to be shown across TV stations and social media in the Republic of Chechnya. The minister was delighted, you could say, with my performance. It was a good performance, I will admit, and assured me that no Chechen would ever harm me now that I'd said sorry. I was saved from the mob. You might think, after an incident like that, that I dislike Chechnya or the Chechen government or Chechens and hold a grudge of some sort, but not at all. We made a deal, me and the Chechens, and they stuck to it. You can't say fairer than that. You apologize, Benjamin, you won't get chucked to the mob. I wasn't chucked to the mob. Fair play, ministers of Chechnya. The minister, who now actually quite liked me after my apology, my kowtowing, as you could say, even invited me back to the ministry building next morning for a chat about helping me make videos in the republic, even dangled the prospect of meeting Ramzan Kadyrov himself, but I wasn't taking any chances, and early the next morning, I snuck out of my hotel and hastily left the republic for the safety of good old Dagestan. Who would have thought that Dagestan would be a place of safety? The experience did show me that, as a YouTuber, you have to be careful about what you say in some countries and some republics. A valuable lesson, which, of course, I almost immediately forgot. My next arrest made headlines far, far, far beyond Chechnya. Akhmat Sila. >> [music] >> For a few years, I'd seen images online of a an abandoned Soviet shuttle, space shuttle, lying in a a hangar on a Russian cosmodrome deep in the middle of the Kazakhstani desert. And in May 2022, myself and my partner in crime, Alinchik, went to Central Asia together, Soviet Central Asia, in search of the Buran. Ooh, the Buran. The journey we took together that dark night through the wild deserts of Kazakhstan was as brutal as well, as brutal as as any I've ever made. Tougher, definitely tougher than crossing the Darian Gap, if you can believe that. I repeatedly fell into animal burrows, these holes that are in the desert made by goodness what animal, and rolled over my dodgy ankles as scorpions and snakes slithered by, trying as hard as I could to suppress my shouts of agony so as not to reveal our location to the guards who patrolled the zone that we were both illegally crossing. And then there was the the mental stress. That was the physical stress, but there was also the mental stress, which was even worse. A local cafe owner had assured us that anyone caught attempting to enter the cosmodrome illegally would automatically receive a 3-month prison sentence. Can you imagine what a flipping Russian gulag prisoners would do to my sweet western cheeks after I would say about 10, 11, 12 grueling hours of walking. Dawn approached, and with the rising sun about to give us away to the guards in the watchtowers, myself and Alinchik found the hangar, the huge hangar. It was massive. I can't explain how big these buildings are on the cosmodrome that that housed the legendary Soviet Buran spacecraft, the most Soviet spacecraft there ever was. Utterly exhausted, of course, after such an adventure. We managed to scale a second-floor window using some scrap metal. You can see all this in the video that we found in some shed. And for the first time, first Alinchik and then I went up and we saw the Buran rotting away in the darkness of the hangar. A moment that I personally dreamed of seeing for years and years and years. It was really the peak moment of the channel. Nothing is beating the bloody Buran. If you want a better video than the Buran, stop watching. It's never happening. Anyway, that should have been the end of the adventure. The plan was just to like rest up for a while and then walk back for another 12 hours to the safety of the road from where we had set off. However, things don't always go as planned. When we descended the window, we saw a patrol car with some Russian policemen speeding towards us. Busted at the Buran. As we walked over to the the cop car, I secretly removed the SIM card. I've had to do it many times in my travels for my camera and put it in my mouth, disguised it, put it in my mouth, where it stayed, believe it or not. My freaking SIM card stayed in my mouth for the next 12 hours. Because to make it all the way there to see the Buran and not have the footage to show for it, well, that would have been I would have killed myself. Myself and Alinchik were both detained for trespass and driven to the nearby city of Baikonur, a closed town, which means it's only open for Russians who worked on a cosmodrome. Quite the privilege and definitely a bucket list moment for a Soviet adventurer like myself to see a closed town, a closed city. At the police station, we were checked in and taken for questioning by some Russian MVD. It's like kind of like Russian police, but a more serious version, who were actually uh friendly towards us and probably quite happy to have something to do for the afternoon. There can't be much action going on in a flipping closed city that is impossible to get into. The whole time I was answering questions, I had the flipping SIM card in my mouth, scared that it would suddenly like fly out cuz I have a habit to spit sometimes when I talk. I don't think, to be honest, the cops really gave too much of a about it all. And in fact, seemed more interested in asking us about how we had avoided the deadly scorpions in the desert during the hike. Incidentally, another Westerner tried to recreate our journey and um he died. So, don't try at home, kids. After a few hours, I don't know, four or five hours of answering questions, we were assured we wouldn't be going to prison, which was a relief. And instead, we're both given a small fine for trespassing on Russian government land. You see how nice the Russians can be? If that was flipping England, imagine what you'd get if you trespass in a cosmodrome. Not that we have cosmodromes. We were driven to a local hotel outside the city walls by a policeman and went to sleep celebrating having gotten away with it all. I could finally take the SIM card out of my bloody mouth, which was a relief. Had it in my cheek all those hours, believe it or not. The adventure was of course not over, otherwise I wouldn't be telling the story. Oh, no. The next morning, we woke up to text messages from just about everyone we knew. Messages like, "Check the news, guys. Check the news." The front page of the Daily Mail, Daily Mail, had run a story about us both being arrested by the Russians. Our passport pages had been leaked to the press and were like everywhere online. They're on Twitter, they're on the news. It was the top story on Sky News, in fact, who made it sound like myself and Alin-Chic were two spies who had been caught and arrested by the Russians and were being sent to the gulag. It was all kind of overexaggerated. It was all overexaggerated. In fact, even the head of the Russian Space Agency had spoken publicly about us and what we were for doing this and then was promptly fired by Putin for the breach of national security. Sorry, Rogozin. If you're watching this, I apologize. You must remember that all this happened when the um or after the war in Ukraine had started. And so, the British press really wanted a big story about a Brit and his mysterious, attractive, Belorussian companion being detained by the enemy. They were no doubt hoping that there'd be some waterboarding and torture, but it wasn't true. The Russians that we met, the police, treated us very well and let us go with the smallest administrative fine that they could actually give us. All very cordial and plenty of handshakes and promises from myself and Alinchik that we would never do that again. And so far we haven't done it again, but you never know, maybe one day I'll do it again. rest and the finding of the baran and everything was like one of the highlights of my life, in fact. I got to see the baran, I got to see inside a closed city, I hung out in a Soviet police station, got interrogated, practiced my Russian. What more could a British gopnik wish for? Not much. The next time I was arrested, however, things wouldn't be um quite so fun. >> [music] >> I'm now going to tell you about my third arrest and well, as most of you will know, by far my most serious situation I've been in as a YouTuber or in fact life in general. In fact, it's a miracle I talked my way out of it, to be honest. When the war in Ukraine started, you may remember I'd left the country on a train packed with refugees who were suddenly leaving behind everything everything they owned, homes, cats, husbands. When I uploaded that video, I wrote my thoughts out underneath it stating my opinion that Putin going to war was a a mad decision. I also posted a link to a UN charity that Ukrainian embassy had asked me to post. They said it was for Ukrainian refugees. Cool. What I didn't know was that doing both of those things was highly illegal in Russia. It meant that I could be in serious trouble if I ever returned to Russia, but who would be stupid enough to go to Russia in the first months of the flipping war? That's right, me. A couple of months later in the summer of 2022, I flew to Siberia to finish a journey I'd started the previous winter. You may remember the Trans-Siberian, St. Petersburg to Vladivostok. This was going to be the culmination of a childhood dream on a trip I was going to show that the politics of Russia and the people of Russia are two entirely separate things. That's always been my goal with my videos and that's what I was going to do, finish that journey and show it. There was a lot of Russophobia at the time. So anyway, I arrived in Birobidzhan in far eastern Siberia to continue where I left off the year previous and began filming again. Birobidzhan is a is an interesting place and on a warm summer's day at least a pleasant place to be and it felt good to be so far away from everything that was happening back in Europe, the total show that was happening in Europe. Whilst filming on the street a pretty policewoman mm approached me and asked me to accompany her back to her apartment. No, back to the police station, which was a little bit unsettling. You see, you never want to be involved in any way with the Russian police when you're in Russia. Just avoid the bloody police in Russia. However, Katya, that was her name, seemed friendly enough and assured me it was just a formality, probably something about my visa and nothing more. The truth was that I'd been traveling, like many tourists who go to Russia, for 2 years across Russia on a a dodgy work visa issued by a shady backstreet visa company with fake invitations and like that from Moscow. I had no real right to have one. I wasn't doing any business. But up to that point I'd gotten away with it. So the worst that would happen for having a dodgy visa, I thought, at the police station would be a maybe a fine. However, when we arrived in the interview room, I saw events were slowly spiraling in an ominous direction. A group of um plainclothes police officers were waiting for me and immediately started asking me questions whether or not I was smuggling anything in the country, what I thought about the war in Ukraine. The The dodgy visa, in fact, that I was like most scared about was hardly mentioned at all. I was then accompanied or asked to accompany the men to the railway hostel where I was staying. And the guy searched everything. When I say everything, I mean bloody everything. They went through my toothpaste tube, inside the cuffs of my shirts. Like they were like checking at the handle of my bag. And at any moment I expected them to pull out like some white powder. Cocaine-um. Cocaine-um. Fortunately, um that didn't happen. Instead, they asked me, I remember it so clearly. They said, um "Bring with you what you think you might need." And I was like, "Need for what?" I mean, I had no idea what was going on. So, um I left all my stuff in the room. I was driven to a gray compound in the center of Birobidzhan city, the Jewish city, in a paddy wagon. And led, to my horror, to a basement room before being stripped of my belt, my shoelaces, like searched everything. I was then had my fingerprints taken and given a rolled-up mattress and an itchy freaking blanket. And um shown to a cell. The heavy Soviet door slammed behind me with an ominous thud. I placed my mattress on one of the empty metal bunk beds, the type which you've seen in a flipping old newsreel from concentration camps, probably Solzhenitsyn or Varlamov slept on one of those bunk beds in the past. And I surveyed my new home, my cell. There was nothing in there. There was just like bloody some sink, a squat toilet, just a hole in the ground, a table and bench that were both cemented into the floor of the cell. A small barred window was positioned really high up, much taller than me, um not much bigger than a letterbox, um but didn't let in much light. The light in the room was provided, in fact, by a a bright bulb above the door um which was never switched off the entire time I was there day or night the entire time I was there this bloody light was just shining in my face. How is a prisoner supposed to goon in private? Of course my mind immediately began racing with questions I had no answers to. Why was I there? How long would I be staying? Would I be able to order a special no cards carbs option for my meals? I don't eat carbs. I had to calm myself as I struggled with the overwhelming feeling of being alone in an underground cell somewhere in the middle of frigging frigging Siberia. I was alone in an underground cell in Siberia. How the do you think I felt? And nobody this is the thing nobody even knew I was there. I didn't tell anyone I didn't get a phone call. I felt like flipping Otto Warmbier. Every now and then the feeding hatch on the door would open a pair of eyes looked into the cell to check I wasn't digging a tunnel or something. Anyway, that night after some dinner of thin watery soup and a chunk of bread. This really was like a day in the life of Ivan Denisovich or Denis Denisovich. Anyway, I lay on my bed bulb shining in my eyes trying to calm my racing mind. Then tomorrow you'll find out what's going on mate. Don't worry about it. Just get through the light. And I was like no matter what the punishment you'll be all right. You'll get through it. I reassured myself but it was just bravado speaking to be honest with you. In reality I felt I might be absolutely  The next morning after a night of zero sleep a prison guard opened the heavy cell door and instructed me to follow him to an office. I was marched down the hall past other cells until eventually I entered a bright office. I was greeted by the governor of the prison a big imposing man a big strong guy this was called Konstantin who had a despite his size he had like a friendly disarming manner which felt really weird in the brutal surroundings. Like how did he end up here? He could have been like a violin teacher or something. But, he was like a big, powerful guy. Anyway, he said, "Benjamin, sit down, please. Some people are here Why was I doing that accent? Some people are here to ask you questions." Was that Jamaican or was that Russian? I don't know. And sitting on the other side of the desk in Konstantin's office were a couple of female officers in blue uniforms. One of the women, when I sat down, slid a piece of paper over the desk for me to look at. It was a screenshot that showed the familiar YouTube logo that we all know so well. And underneath it was my Ukraine video and what I'd written about the war. I said to myself. And it was obvious then that it wasn't just about a flipping dodgy visa. "Did you write this?" one of the officers asked. "Yes," I replied. There was no point lying. I mean, it was my bloody video. Some papers were shuffled by the girls. "We are charging you under the Russian Federation" Should I stop doing the not the Russian accent? I think so. They said, "We're charging you under the Russian Federation's criminal code for the offense of offending the president of Russia and the special military operation." I've been offending two people, flipping heck. And the crime of raising [clears throat] money for the enemy. That freaking thing, the army, the Ukrainian army, the Ukrainian government, the Ukrainian embassy asked me to bloody post. I signed a piece of paper saying I understood the charges. Although, of course, I didn't understand anything. It was all in bloody Russian. And with that, was led back to my cell. As the um door of my cell slammed behind me, I really went into bloody panic mode, to be honest with you. Pacing the gray tomb, struggling to control all the emotions inside of me. How much trouble was I in? Would I be held by the Russians for years and then be traded like the big old lesbian Brittney Griner? An hour later, my cell door was opened and it was Konstantin, the governor. He led me back to his office and put the kettle on. "Benjamin, take a seat," he said as he stirred the instant coffee and handed it to me. Then he gave a little speech. Oh, yeah, I remember the speech very well, even to this day. I'm going to do it in my English accent. It's ridiculous trying to do it in a Russian accent. He said, "My son serves in the SMO, with the special forces. A lot of boys have gone to Ukraine from this region to fight. Some do not come back, but we believe we are fighting a just war for Russia and the people of Donbas. What you said online insults everyone risking their lives and their families waiting for them to return." When he said that, I genuinely felt bad. I liked Konstantin, and despite what I thought personally about the war, he was a father who didn't know if his son would ever return. I took a slurp of the coffee, I remember it was nasty Nescafe, cheap coffee, and said, "Konstantin, prosti menya." And I meant it. Means I'm sorry in Russian. He opened a packet of biscuits and placed them on a plate in front of me to show that there were no hard feelings. And then he said, "Benjamin, in an hour, someone is going to come and visit you. They're going to ask you some questions, and if you want to leave this prison and return home, answer them carefully. Think about your answers." Ominous. But I understood fully what big Konstantin was asking of me. I returned to my cell with a cup of noodle a cup of noodles, Doshirak, Konstantin had made for me, and waited to see what would happen next. An hour later, my cell door opened again, and I was led up some stairs to a small office overlooking the prison yard, where some prisoners were like smoking. You know, like proper Russian prisons, how you imagine it. And in his office, there was a video camera. I was like, "For sake, what's going on here?" "I'm here to ask you some questions to help the judge decide your case in the trial later today." "What exactly is the punishment going to be?" I asked, not sure I wanted to hear the man's answer. "It depends on what the judge decides, but based on your answer the questions, how you answer the question. Anything from a fine or up to 7 years in prison. 7 years in freaking prison. That was almost as long as you get in the UK for tweeting something about illegal freaking migrants. 7 years I could have been doing in Siberia. In those moments, you have a choice. You can be a Navalny type character and you can be a hero and die in a cold prison camp in the Arctic or you can say whatever people want to hear and go back to your nice life. Have a nice life here. Check out my apartment. Why would I swap it for a prison? I ain't getting no poison frog rubbed on me. The thing that was that I was assured that whatever I said on camera would strictly was strictly for the court and would never be made public. Yeah, unlike the Chechens, it seems you can't trust the freaking Russian government when you strike a deal. Anyway, later on in my clothes that smelled of the damp prison cell, I was driven to a courthouse in central Bilibino, the central courthouse where I stood before a female judge who had a severe Soviet kind of demeanor. Kind of sexy. I Alla Pugacheva. Sexy but severe. When it was my turn to speak after the prosecution had laid out their case, I turned on every ounce of charm I possessed inside of me using words I learned in my learned in my very first Russian classes with Anna, some will remember her, some years before, but I never thought I would ever need. I apologized for my stupidity and played the dumb Englishman who knew nothing of geopolitics, which I mean, that's basically true. I'm a dummy. What do I know? I told her also how much I loved Russia, but preferred it on the outside of the prison rather than inside, which made the judge giggle. And when she giggled, I knew everything was going to be okay. You can't giggle and then send a man to 7 years in prison. So, anyway, as the female judge retired to her chamber to decide my punishment, I waited in the corridor pacing around whilst a guard was like watching me in case I was going to escape. But where the you'd run to from Birobidzhan? I mean, Birobidzhan is the Zhopa Mira as they say in Russia, the ass end of the world. So, there's nowhere to run to. Then I remember Konstantin the prison warden came in, patted me on the shoulder and said, "Ben, don't worry, everything's going to be okay." And so, I knew I'd be all right. The court was called back into session, I stood in the dock waiting to hear the verdict. "You have been found guilty of breaking the criminal code in regards to the defamation of the Russian president and the SMO. I am finding you 50,000 rubles." The judge said, which sounds like a shitload, right? 50,000 rubles, but it's actually I think it's like $400 or something. I let a little sigh of relief. Then she paused and said, "And 3 years exclusion from the territory of the Russian Federation." "Bleen!" I exclaimed like just inadvertently from the dock, and the judge raised her eyebrows and giggled again. My journeys across the former USSR were now finally over. And I suppose to all intents and purposes with my channel, it'd been a hell of a journey. And yeah, that's it. Those are the three most I was going to say most popular arrests, but they're not popular, are they? Write in the comment section which was your favorite story, which one would you would have yourself the most >> [laughter] >> if you'd been in that situation. And um thank you for watching. Dasviduli. No, not dasviduli. We're in Prague for God's sakes. Natsikladna. Dicky dicky.
